frameworks.name: deep recurrent neural network (RNN)
frameworks.one_line: RNN trained on magnitude comparison serves as model system alongside humans

frameworks.name: neural state space / population geometry
frameworks.one_line: representational geometry of parallel context-specific number lines analyzed in brain and RNN

frameworks.name: neural normalisation
frameworks.one_line: normalisation supports cross-context alignment of magnitude codes

frameworks.name: abstract relational generalisation
frameworks.one_line: framework for transferring 'more/less' relations across physical scales

core_question: How do humans and recurrent networks represent magnitude so that relational knowledge generalises across contexts on different physical scales?
key_assumptions: magnitude comparison ('more/less') is a simple abstract relation
human brain signals and RNN hidden unit activity are comparable via multivariate analysis
contexts can share relational structure while differing in physical scale
manipulations: context defining the physical scale of magnitudes to be compared
comparison of human neural signals vs. trained RNN hidden activity
key_findings: both humans and RNNs developed parallel neural 'number lines' for each context
the per-context number state spaces were aligned across contexts in both systems
alignment was structured to explicitly facilitate generalisation of 'more' and 'less'
interpretations: neural normalisation plays a previously overlooked role in supporting transfer of abstract relational knowledge
shared geometric alignment is a common mechanism for magnitude generalisation in brains and machine learning systems
connecting_signals: recurrent neural network model of cognition
